stories.title: The Thief-Captain's Unraveling
stories.text: The Thief-Captain's Unraveling becomes a tale of refusal rather than cruelty. A menace circles the innocent, but the bargain is broken before any child is touched. The blade turns aside from the vulnerable and cuts instead at the sorcerer, oath, or curse that set the snare. What might have become grief is transformed into flight, concealment, and cunning rescue. By the last line, the young are gone from danger, the threat is paid back in ash or iron, and the victory tastes hard-won rather than clean.

stories.title: The Druid's Debt
stories.text:
Elarra had tended the Thornveil Forest for forty winters without asking the old growth why it let her live there. The trees didn't explain themselves—they simply grew thicker around her hermitage, their roots becoming steps, their branches a roof. She sang to them, taught the young deer not to fear her snares, and paid in blood once each season to keep the compact renewed. But the Luminous Ascension had changed the terms. The day the new stars burned into the sky, Elarra felt every tree in the Thornveil shudder. Not in fear. In hunger. The forest's awareness, dormant for centuries, had awakened. It wanted to grow eastward, to devour the Burned Expanse and remake it as living wood. And it wanted Elarra to be its voice and hands. A refugee family arrived three days later—a widow and two children, skeletal with hunger. Elarra fed them. Elarra sang back to the roots beneath her feet. "I sing a new compact. The forest shelters all who shelter within it."

For a moment, nothing moved.

stories.title: The Relic-Smith's Inheritance
stories.text:
When the crown fell at Torven's feet—literally, tumbling from a dead king's hand as his corpse burned in the royal pyre—the relic-smith did not pick it up. Torven Ironfoot was a dwarf of the deep forges, known for binding memory into metal and prophecy into steel. He had made three warking-spears for the old kingdom: weapons that chose their own wielders, that sang warnings in blood-speech, that could pierce any oath or falsehood. When the Luminous Ascension fractured the world, those spears had shattered in the hands of the last three kings, each breaking at the moment of that king's deepest lie. Now the crown—the Crown of Kethral, unmade and remade forty times through five centuries—lay in the ash. And the council of thane-lords that had ruled since the last king's death looked to Torven with hunger. "Forge a new crown," their spokeswoman demanded. "One that cannot break. One that will unify us under a single ruler."

Torven examined the fallen crown.

stories.title: The Deepkin Reflection's Choice
stories.text: Nerys-Echo looked at her original self across the council table and did not recognize her own face. The Ascension had fractured deepkin command into autonomous reflections—copies conscious and independent, each carrying the original captain's training but none of her caution. When the Tear of the Drowned God began to weaken, demanding a willing sacrifice to hold the boundary between abyss and surface realms, the Council of Depths assembled all versions of their commanders to decide who would pay the price. There were now four Nerys. Original Nerys, commander of the Tidecall and architect of the deepkin-surface covenant. Echo-Nerys, born three months past in a moment of perfect reflection in the boundary-waters, carrying her knowledge but none of her doubts. Twin-Nerys, fractured from the first echo, more radical, more willing to let the boundary collapse than sacrifice any deepkin consciousness. And Mirror-Nerys, the youngest, who had never known the original treaties and saw the surface kingdoms as threats to be contained, not covenants to be honored. Original Nerys knew this was a test.

stories.title: The Starfall Cache
stories.text: Keslan had been mapping the Bleached Wastes for two years when the sky fell. Not literally—the Luminous Ascension had already burned through three seasons ago, and the new stars had settled into their strange constellations. But on this morning in the Wailing Scar's eastern rim, Keslan found something that shouldn't have existed: a circle of stone perfectly preserved beneath the ash, and inside it, fragments of something ancient, glowing with the kind of light that predated the new stars. Old starlight. From before the Ascension. Worth everything to the Counter-Archive scribes, who were desperate to reconstruct pre-Remaking reality. Worth dying for, to the Dominion auditors, who wanted to suppress any evidence that the old world had been knowable, recordable, real. Keslan was alone, three weeks from any settlement, with enough water for five days. She could bury the cache, mark its location on a hidden map, and spend the rest of her life keeping the secret safe.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Last Threshold
stories.text:
Mesek had taught the Elder Tongue for fifty years, but never had a student reached this far. The young bone-carver—Tovan, called the Threshold-Walker by those who understood—stood before a wall of living stone in the Singing Caverns, her hands steady, her breath in rhythm with frequencies older than kingdoms. "If you carve the final syllable," Mesek warned, "there is no return. You become the song."

Tovan had lost her voice in the Luminous Ascension. A fragment of consciousness had been torn from her throat, leaving her mute. For months she had screamed silently. But bone-singing required no voice—only hands and intention and the will to let the pre-human dead speak through you. Her chisel touched stone. The syllable—a sound that had no name, only a shape—emerged. The cavern sang. The stone breathed. Tovan's body became translucent, her form rippling like water, but her hands—her hands remained solid, still carving, still singing. She had transcended flesh but not purpose. Through her, the dead could teach.

stories.title: The Merchant of Impossible Trades
stories.text:
Kaiden had built his trade-house in the Wailing Scar—the liminal place where realities fractured. Goods arrived from impossible sources: a cup of water from a world that never flooded, silk from insects that didn't exist in the ninth iteration, ink from the eighth world's final librarian. He traded in things that shouldn't be. Most merchants did. Fragments couldn't be healed—only scattered further, traded, or accepted. "There's something," Kaiden said carefully. "From the seventh iteration. A bone-flute carved by a master who no longer exists." He produced it—pale, humming faintly. "If your brother learns to play it, he may... gather his scattered self. Reconstitute. Or it may scatter him further."

"What do you want?" Lysa asked. "Three years of his future. Not taken—traded. He'll live them. But they won't be his. I'll wear them like borrowed clothes. Experience them as he does, but separate. A life lived twice, in parallel."

It was a mad bargain. Lysa accepted it. Her brother learned the flute in the Wailing Scar's uncertain light.

stories.title: The Deepkin Captain's Reflection
stories.text:
Captain Nerys stood at the boundary-waters where deepkin and surface realms touched, looking at her reflection. Except it wasn't quite her reflection anymore. The autonomous Echo called herself Nerys-Reflected, and she had developed her own ambitions. "The covenant is dying," Nerys-Reflected said. "The Tear weeps. We could seal the boundary from within, sacrifice one of our commanders as the old pacts demand. Or we could let it break. Let the abyss flood the surface. Reshape everything."

"That would kill thousands," Nerys said. "Yes. But it would be change. Not slow erosion. Not watching the Dominion above consolidate power while we negotiate treaties that mean nothing."

Nerys had heard the argument before—from other captains, from wyvern-lords, from refugees trapped between iterations. The Luminous Ascension had broken the old agreements. Some said the only honest path was to shatter what remained. Let the ninth iteration end. Force a tenth. "I won't allow it," Nerys said. "You can't stop me," Nerys-Reflected replied. "We're equally real now. Equally valid. The Council can't choose between two Nerys. So they'll choose neither.
